onlyOne.note: Vietnamese can be typed without tone marks, which Vietnamese speakers can usually guess depending on context — Wikipedia: Vietnamese language and computers
The classifier 'con' generally introduces animate objects, especially animals, e.g. 'con gà' (chicken), 'con chó' (dog) — Wikipedia: Vietnamese grammar
classifiers are frequently required when a noun is accompanied by a numeral; in Vietnamese the classifier always precedes the noun but comes after the number — Wikipedia: Classifier (linguistics)
the last syllable in a primary name with a title before it is used to call or address a person: 'ông Dũng', 'anh Dũng' — Wikipedia: Vietnamese name
Chữ quốc ngữ was created by Portuguese missionaries according to the phonemic orthography of Portuguese language — Wikipedia: Vietnamese alphabet
The velar stop is written variously as 'c', 'k', or 'q' in Vietnamese — Wikipedia: Vietnamese phonology / Talk:Vietnamese phonology
Vietnamese lacks morphological markings of case, gender, number, and tense (and, as a result, has no finite/nonfinite distinction) — Wikipedia: Vietnamese morphology
whereas European languages tend to use morphology to express tense, Vietnamese uses grammatical particles or syntactic constructions — Wikipedia: Vietnamese grammar
the exclusive use of the Vietnamese words for 'I' and 'you' are considered informal and rude — Wikipedia: Pronouns in Vietnamese
Vietnamese is an isolating language (highly analytic)...Vietnamese morphemes and phonological words are predominantly monosyllabic — Wikipedia: Vietnamese language / Vietnamese morphology

'dạ' combines with 'vâng' to form 'dạ vâng,' a polite affirmative response, particularly used when addressing elders or in formal/respectful contexts — Wikipedia: Vietnamese grammar / Pronouns in Vietnamese
Vietnamese pronouns indicate the social status between speakers and other persons in the discourse in addition to grammatical person and number — Wikipedia: Pronouns in Vietnamese

a pronoun usually connotes a degree of family relationship or kinship, and in polite speech...kinship terminology is used when referring to oneself, the audience, or a third party — Wikipedia: Pronouns in Vietnamese
Tôi is often used formally...conveys equal status; tao, mày...commonly used in familiar social contexts...otherwise considered impolite — Wikipedia: Vietnamese language / Pronouns in Vietnamese
Languages that use many modal particles in their spoken form include Vietnamese — Wikipedia: Sentence-final particle / Modal particle
Vietnamese orthography uses spaces to separate syllables rather than words — Wikipedia: Vietnamese language
the first tone is not marked and the other five are indicated by diacritics applied to the vowel part of the syllable — Wikipedia: Vietnamese phonology
Standard Vietnamese has six tones (ngang, sắc, huyền, hỏi, ngã, nặng); modern linguists count 6 phonemic tones — Wikipedia: Vietnamese phonology
Vietnamese is a tonal language, so the meaning of each word depends on the pitch in which it is pronounced — Wikipedia: Vietnamese language
postpositive adjectives are the norm: it is normal for an attributive adjective to follow, rather than precede, the noun it modifies — Wikipedia: Postpositive adjective / Vietnamese grammar
Vietnamese has a basic subject-verb-object (SVO) word order, though utterances may be restructured to be topic-prominent — Wikipedia: Vietnamese grammar
many multisyllabic words do occur, usually as a result of compounding and reduplication — Wikipedia: Vietnamese morphology
Vietnamese orthography uses spaces to separate syllables rather than words, meaning multisyllabic words frequently appear as separate tokens — Wikipedia: Vietnamese language
